engineering.translation: The chapter's most metaphysically loaded verse. Actions are done by the gunas of prakṛti; the ahaṅkāra-vimūḍha — whose self-conception is fooled by ego — claims kartāham (I am the doer). Stay at the operational layer. The doctrinal claim about the Self's non-doership exceeds what the analog can carry, but its operational shadow is real. Engineering map: when an engineer takes sole credit for what a team's joint practice produced, that is the operational version of the kartāham misreading. The review that caught the bug, the on-call shift that surfaced the regression, the design meeting that reshaped the proposal, the historical context one staff member brought, the institutional memory carried by another — all of those threads wove the shipped artifact. Saying 'I shipped this' reads woven production as solo authorship. Tagged STRETCHED: the doctrine is heavier than this map.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior engineer at a Kubernetes-heavy infrastructure firm merges a major migration on GitHub on a Friday afternoon. Datadog reports the system stable; Slack lights up with congratulations. He drafts a retrospective in Notion in the tone of 'I shipped this.' His writeup omits: the staff engineer who, six months earlier in a design review, redirected the proposal away from a class of failure modes; the SRE on-call who caught a config bug three weeks ago that would have cascaded; the principal who silently re-reviewed the PR at midnight Wednesday and left twelve comments that materially reshaped the implementation; the docs two retired engineers wrote six years earlier that carried the institutional memory of why the cluster-controller exists in its current form. None of those threads appear. He thinks of the merge as his merge. The verse names exactly this: prakṛter guṇaiḥ kriyamāṇāni karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ — actions are done by the gunas in every respect — ahaṅkāra-vimūḍhātmā kartāham iti manyate — the ego-fooled self thinks 'I am the doer.' At the operational shadow: a team's joint practice produced the output; reading that as solo authorship is the same misreading.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if read as 'never take credit.' The verse's operational shadow is not 'reject all attribution.' It is 'attribute the action to its actual sources, including the parts of the production that lie outside your direct authorship.' The diagnostic: does the retrospective name at least three contributors whose contribution materially shaped the outcome? If yes, the kartāham misreading is being avoided. If no, the verse names the writer.
engineering.counter_example: Sometimes the action is genuinely individual — the engineer who, alone over six weeks, with no review and no help, debugs a deeply opaque issue. Even here the verse's operational shadow holds at the meta-layer (the system's documentation, the build infrastructure, the chain of code reviews on prior PRs that produced the maintainable codebase he is debugging) — but at the immediate-credit layer the individual contribution is real. The verse does not erase real authorship; it warns against the operational habit of reading collective production as individual authorship.
engineering.implication: On the next shipping retrospective you write, list explicitly the five contributors whose contribution shaped the outcome — including those whose contribution was historical, structural, or invisible. If the list is hard to fill, the kartāham misreading has already happened.
engineering.quotable_line: The team's collective practice produced the artifact. The 'I shipped this' framing is the operational version of the verse's kartāham misreading.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation for BG 3.27: source pack triangulated across 5 sources; engineering layer anchored on chapter thesis (Why the Senior Still Ships) and load-bearing claims for this verse. Tagged STRETCHED: the metaphysical claim of 3.27 (gunas as the actual agent of action; the Self's non-doership) exceeds what the engineering analog can carry. The operational shadow (collective production misread as personal authorship) is honest but more limited than the doctrinal claim.
